speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number two, August 15th, 1995, interview with survivor Catheryne Morgan. So can you tell me a little bit more about [INAUDIBLE]? Am I pronouncing it right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, pretty well.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What-- tell me again a typical day. Maybe how many hours you might have slept, what time you wake up in the morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- I wouldn't know. All I know that we were herded out early in the morning, and we stayed outdoors the whole blessed day. It was already winter because, obviously, in Poland it starts already in October, and this is November and December. And we went out to this area, which was quite a way, incidentally, from the camp itself. And we were doing this work. And I don't know how on earth did we manage even to lift the shovel, or we had wheelbarrows, some girl's had. And we were taking out earth. And then we were herded back to camp. And just what time it was, I haven't got the slightest idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they get you from one place to the other?

speaker: interviewee
text: By foot. They marched us. And of course, the dogs were are all around, the infamous shepherds, and schnell, schnell. And they marched us to the work site. I don't think it was too far away, but I really don't have any recollection.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were with your Aunt Rose this whole time.

speaker: interviewee
text: All, my Aunt Rose and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the languages that were going on around you? Could you talk to other girls there, understand?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we could. We had a lot of Polish girls. That's when I picked up a lot of Polish from my Czech. But we had also Hungarian, so it was-- I would say it was Czech, Polish, and Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any acts of kindness in this camp? People saving bread for each other, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the only thing I remember, it was Hanukkah, and one of the girls had a potato. She dug it out. Then she find some oil, and she lit a Hanukkah candle. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But there was great solidarity, and there was no-- I never heard any arguments, or yelling, or any at all. But most of all, I remember, it must have been Hanukkah and it, obviously, was Christmastime, because we could see from our barracks where the German lived. And it was lit up with candles. And some beautiful smells of pastry came around.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we, with big eyes, we were looking. Will we ever be in a warm and clean place ourself? That's about the only thing I remember of it, so it must have been December. What-- what kept you going? What sustained you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hard to say. I really don't know, because I don't think our thinking was absolutely clear, to say the least. We were already starving. And it was just mechanically, that we could get up, and do, and come. And I don't think we gave any philosophical thought to anything-- at least, not in my case. I was a kid, and I was emaciated. I started to look like a child. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And up until the day that we left, and all I remembered, they herded us to these columns, and we were on foot. And we left [INAUDIBLE] on the infamous death march, which eventually led, then, to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: The shoe-- we had, like, straw, and some girls had wooden shoes. What I had, I haven't got the slightest idea. But whatever it was, it obviously wasn't much, because during the march, we would-- they would herd us at night, usually, into-- must have been horse's stalls, because it was full of straw. And we would sleep there. And we would take some of that straw, and I know that one of the [INAUDIBLE] would come, and my feet were frozen. And Aunt Rose took my feet in her mouth to warm them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the next morning, they would send the dogs again, because a lot of girls try to hide in the straw. They-- incidentally, while we were marching, even during the day, those who couldn't, they were shot on the spot and left in the ditches. So we were dwindling down. But still, in the morning, the dogs would come and search for those who tried to hide. And then things were-- got from bad to worse, if there is such a thing. And my aunt said that she cannot go on. And she knew that she would be shot, and she must hide. And luckily, she spoke a very beautiful German. When she was a young girl, they had a nanny from Germany, and so she spoke a perfect German. And she said she just must, because she knows. And she blessed me to go on, and I did, indeed. And miraculously, the dogs didn't find her, and she hid. Later, I find out she got out. And she was hiding with a German family. She told them she was bombed out of Dresden, and she was hiding with them until the-- practically the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that separation like, with your aunt? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it happened during the night. And all I remember, that she said she cannot. And she knows-- and she told me, too-- if I can, I should, because neither did I look German, of course, and my German was very poor. So I couldn't have hid, really, and pretended that I'm one. And she felt that I must go on. And so I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it shocking to you, all this death around you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't a shock anymore. You became numb.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when it became a routine and not-- was it in Auschwitz that you saw death for the first time or--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Auschwitz that I saw death, yeah. People collapsed around us, and they told us they were dead. And we were touched by, somehow you become totally numb. And we only saw-- we heard the shots on the death march, but I never saw the-- actually, the execution itself. But we knew perfectly well that whoever couldn't continue was shot on the spot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long do you think you were on this death march?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. It must have been weeks and weeks. All I rem-- the only recollection I have, just before Bergen-Belsen, we-- they sent us through a frozen lake. And we were guarded, incidentally, not by SS-- by Wehrmacht. And I must say, some of them were quite humane. And in fact, one of us told us when we said, we are so dirty. And [INAUDIBLE] said, don't worry. One day you will look beautiful again. Which was truly the only kindness that I ever heard. But the-- on this frozen lake, instead of the Wehrmacht [INAUDIBLE], they send us first, to see whether the ice will hold us. And we went through this frozen lake. And the next thing I know, we were in Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: On this death march, were you fed at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Obviously, somehow, somewhere-- how, I have no recollection. But I know we were dreadfully hungry. And to this day, my husband always laughs because if I'm on the street, and I see a piece of fruit lying on the [INAUDIBLE], especially if that was a core of an apple. We saw it, and we plunged at it. It was the delicacy of the day. So we must have been emaciated and terrifically starved, by then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what the death march was like. Was it a couple girls across, walking?

speaker: interviewee
text: We weren't any more in rows. It was-- it was almost, if you want to call it, disorderly. There were so few of us left, and it was like a group of women, just dragging themselves, literally. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It wasn't a march, like you would imagine soldiers marching, obviously. And how many were left, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you remember, when you finally got to Bergen-Belsen, your impressions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I saw immediately the barracks, which reminded us of Auschwitz. And they herded us to the barracks, which were dreadful because there was a typhoid epidemic. And then I saw dead bodies actually piled up, already, there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember, what time of year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was spring, already. It was beginning of spring.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they assigned you to barracks, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: They left us in the barracks. Then one day, with all that I must tell you, that my vanity never left me, because dirty and emaciated as we were, I took some snow and I would wash myself and, quote unquote, do my hair, which was non-existent. And somehow, one day they came and they chose about a dozen girls. We didn't know why, and I was among them. And that was my salvation. They took us out of Bergen-Belsen. Nearby, there was a camp. Obviously, the German lived there. I don't know the name of it. And we were doing the housework for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So conditions have greatly improved for me. They probably-- food-wise, I have no recollection. But I know that it was certainly not Bergen-Belsen. We didn't see any dead bodies, and we were living under relatively better conditions. What were the conditions like? Were you in a house, or a--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like barracks. It was barracks for the German personnel. And we did some cleaning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what kind of cleaning? Did you have to do kitchens or-- no?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I don't recall. I know it was some sort of a house work, and I don't recall when we were fed and how. But I know it was out-- and we were not guarded constantly. And it was beautiful. I saw the forest, and I saw the flowers already budding. And was great longing, that how come I'm just an inch away, and I'm not free.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you do that for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Must have been for a number of weeks, until one day we were taken back to Bergen-Belsen. And that must have been a day or two before April 15th, Sunday, 1945, when we were liberated by the British. A day or two before, we were taken back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any conversations with some of these girls that you had met, about the war ending? Had they heard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were-- yes, and many of them knew that the war is ending. But we also heard dreadful rumors that they are putting glass in our bread, because they don't want anyone to find alive. And we were hearing all kind of rumors, what they were doing as a last-minute, last-ditch effort to erase all traces. But I have not, personally, seen any-- anyone shot there. I only saw, again, the typhoid. My father, luckily, before we left, had us inoculated against typhoid. Maybe that's why I didn't get it. But just about 99% of people did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you afraid to eat the bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, when we heard about the glass, yes. Yes, we were afraid to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what-- just, can you describe what it was like? You were separated, now, from your Aunt Rose. You had no--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no one and I thought of her. But I tell you, it's such a dull and dreadful feeling that comes over you. And as I'm reading now, more and more about it-- what it does to the physical body, to the brain itself, to be deprived of food-- I had no recollection of any emotions. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I also remember that somebody said that they saw my sister. So I went into separate barracks and called her name. But unfortunately, I didn't. I did find out that there was somebody who my father and mother were friends with, a Doctor Hoffman, whose wife was there. And indeed, I was the last one to speak with her. And to my eternal delight, I found her son, who was a doctor in Sweden. And I was able to tell him that I was with his mother just before she died. But that's the only person that I met in Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, these rumors that you heard, that the war was ending, feelings of hope and wonder, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Funny, none-- it were just, it will end and I will be with my parents. But there was no elation, not even on the day of liberation. I can't say that. We were not euphoric, and nobody was dancing in the street. We either didn't have the strength or the stamina to do either. The British came in, and they were extremely kind. They treated us like children and they were gentle and kind. But as I said, there was no joy. And I was really too sick and emaciated. And they took me, immediately, to near Bergen-Belsen to another place-- not where we worked, where the Germans used to live. And we were in very clean and nice barracks, assigned to, like, five girls to one cubicle. And it was clean and nice. And the girls would go out. But I was just-- I always try to analyze it. I must have been in a state of terrific depression, because I wouldn't eat. Even after the liberation, I couldn't. And the girls were extremely nice. And they went out and got food and brought me. And I was just lying there, I remember, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for a long, long period of time. And I just wouldn't take any nourishment, afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember feeling that you wanted to die?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I was always hoping to see my family. And in fact, I never forget, one kind British soldier came, and he brought us stuff. And he gave me a box of sardines. [CRYING] And my sister was very fond of sardines. And I hid it, to bring it home for her. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was so-- we were so hopeful that, you know, this is all a bad dream, and they're waiting for us at home. We just didn't realize that-- right then and there, we didn't realize that, indeed, what happened to them. And if it did, we didn't want to face it, obviously. So with that hope, we all tried to reach home, to see our families again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how long you were living in these barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too long. A goodly few weeks, but not more than that, until one day they took us-- those of us who could go. And obviously, I walked on my own. Otherwise, I would have been taken to Sweden, as so many of the very ill, as you might know. And we were herded-- I mean, asked to come in these open trucks. And they took us to Czechoslovakia. Actually, the first stop was Pilsen, I remember. And that's where I got my first identity. I was a name, again. And then to Prague, where we got Red Cross pass-- passports from the Red Cross, that I was a displaced person. My name, such and such. And from there, whoever wanted to leave, where. And by a roundabout way, and I have absolutely no recollection how, I got to Budapest, again, to one of these areas where all the people who were liberated were. And there were posted names. And of course, I was anxious to see whether any of my family would be on it. But I didn't find them. But I did find an aunt in Budapest who survived the war there. She was a distant aunt, not a real aunt. And she was very nice to me, during these few days or perhaps a few weeks that I spent in Budapest, before I went to Czechoslovakia. And again, just how I got there, I have no idea. I find myself back in Czechoslovakia, and I heard rumors that my mother is there. Well, they mixed her up with my aunt, because my aunt's husband was also a pharmacist. And they said, the pharmacist's wife. And to my great delight, then, I found my aunt. She was waiting, yes, she was. But she was the only one who has returned. And she's the one, then, who took care of me, literally, like an infant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your aunt was-- was she older than your mother or younger?

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt was actually two years younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she had survived by staying with this German family?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the German family, up until the time when the Russian approached and they had to flee, themselves. And she went, right away, to the Russians, who brought her, then, within borders of Czechoslovakia. And she doesn't know what happened to the family. To this day, she is not sure whether they know that she was-- she has a feeling-- he was a school director, very lovely, gentle people, with a daughter. And she always has a feeling that they knew who she was, but they pretended not to. And while they had a lot of hardship, themselves-- by then, there was hardly any food and ration-- they shared with her, everything. And she managed to stay with them, as I said, until the Russians were right at the door, so to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember ever asking her why this was happening to the Jewish people? Did you wonder-- here you-- [INAUDIBLE] you weren't-- you were very assimilated Jews, and yet this was happening to you. Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't ask her, because it was just happened. We just knew that they, for some reason, we were not wanted, hated, and they wanted to annihilate us. But I didn't ask her. I wasn't a [INAUDIBLE] child that would ask mommy why. I just knew that it's because I'm a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was the reunion like, with your Aunt Rose?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was fantastic, of course. And she tried to get back some of our possessions-- at least so we can stay. But she was unsuccessful. Still, she managed, somehow, we stayed for a while. And all I remember, that she was managed to get me some food and shelter, of course, for the both of us. And it took weeks and weeks until I was able to even function. And all I remembered, that I would wash myself, always, in the dark. I couldn't look at myself, because as I can encircle my wrist, here, that's how I used to encircle my arm. I was absolutely a skeleton. And I looked like a seven-year-old child. And then, when I got better, she wanted to get out of there, as fast as possible. We never asked back to get our personal things, nor our possessions. The only thing she was able to get from the mayor of the town, who certified that, indeed, we lived there, and my father owned a pharmacy, and with that, we left. And she made up her mind, where we want to be is in Rome, in Italy. Her son was there, and that was a safe place. And that's where we headed, a roundabout way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get to Rome? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went, first, to Bratislava, which was the capital of Slovakia. No sooner did we get there, I was back in the hospital. I got jaundiced, and I was quite ill for weeks and weeks, in the hospital in Bratislava. Then when I got better, somehow-- it must have been the government-- sent us, which was very nice, to the [INAUDIBLE] mountains, to recuperate. And we stayed there for a couple of weeks. And the minute I got better, we started a journey to-- through Vienna. How we got to Vienna, I do not know. But we got to Vienna, and we got lodging with some people. And she went to ask where the-- well, it wasn't the joint distribution. It was some kind of a committee. And she asked how we can manage to stay, or how we can get out of Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: So obviously, they must have assisted us, after Vienna. Because we went to a DP-lager, which was near Linz. It used to be, probably, a military barrack. And we stayed there for a few weeks. And there lots of DPs from all over. And then they took us to a lovely place called Bad Gastein, which is in Austria, near Salzburg-- and where the hotels were converted for the DPs to stay.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's my nicest recollection. We stayed there, and it was warm, and clean, and lovely. It was middle of the winter, by then. And we stayed there. And then, through the help of the Haganah-- [INAUDIBLE] as you might know-- we were all going to Israel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they transported us, as I always like to say, with a [INAUDIBLE] on a convertible-- in an open truck, they took us-- middle of the night. And how they managed to take us through-- there was some Russian territory, too. And they took us, smuggled us, into Italy. And it was beautiful. One morning, we wake up and it was the balmy, beautiful weather of Italian Alps. And we landed in Bolzano, then, later, Murano, and the final destination, then, Milano. And we were in Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- Did you speak Italian at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't, but I studied Latin. And somehow, I picked it up. At first, very funny, but in no time at all, I learned. Later on-- I stayed, of course, in Italy for a number of years. And I perfected it. But somehow, we manage. And I just want to say that the Italians were the most lovely, the most warm-hearted, compassionate, and delightful people you can imagine. They were kind and nice, and they really closed an eye. They knew we are there illegally, yet they never made any distinction between food stamps. I believe they still had-- this is, after all, right after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to Milano, and from there we went to Rome. That I remember, by train-- now, how she managed to get train tickets, I don't know. Because during all this time, our total possession were three single dollar bills. That was between my aunt and I.

speaker: interviewee
text: How she got them, she must have sold-- she had some things with her-- a camera, something. She must have sold some of her stuff. And we got to Rome , because where her son was, and that's the only family that we knew was in Rome. So we landed, indeed, in Rome.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that reunion like, with your cousin?

speaker: interviewee
text: With her son? I suppose, very warm and very great, and the Italian family were extremely nice. And we stayed with them a very short time. But after that, we were sort of made-- feel a bit uncomfortable, on their part. And I must add, not to the Italian, the real Italian people, who was a lovely grandmother and her son, to whom this aunt was married to. And he was a high-ranking officer in the Naval ministry. And they were as nice as can be. But somehow, the two aunts, they objected that we stay there that length of time. And one of them, my aunt then-- later on in years-- told me that she complained that I took two pieces of bread in the morning. And so my aunt said, time to leave. And we find out, roundabout way, that there is a girl kibbutz, again, through the Haganah, obviously, in [INAUDIBLE], which is near Rome. And all those who want to go to Israel can stay there, in this girl kibbutz. So we moved. And indeed, we stayed there, then, for a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this, that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was only 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was 1946 that you first made to Italy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Right, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your aunt moved into the [INAUDIBLE] kibbutz with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. The two of us, always together-- there were about four or five girls to each room-- my aunt and two or three other girls. And it was a real kibbutz life. We all worked and did whatever we can. And we were supposed to go several times, they had [INAUDIBLE] to Israel. Fortunately or unfortunately, each time we were ready to go, I became ill again. I was more in the hospital in Rome than I was in Austria. I was in and out of the hospital. And I missed all the opportunity to go. I knew I had an uncle in New York. His name is Daniel. It was Daniel that was my father's only brother-- only other brother, besides Sigmund. But as a child, I didn't know where. And of course, they corresponded. But I had no idea where. So somebody once said, why don't you ask someone to give an announcement in a paper. And somebody must have done it for me, obviously, in Austria. Might have been the Jewish agency, I really don't know. But after a few weeks, I got a beautiful letter. Not from my uncle, but a man who-- and his wife who, I hope, will eternally be rewarded in heaven. [CRYING] Mr. And Mrs. Phillip Hautzman of Detroit. And they wrote me a letter that, while we are not your uncle, we want to help you. And you are not related to us, but we want to send you an affidavit. We have three children of our own, and we want you to come and be our daughter. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So practically like by return mail, they send me the papers. But of course, it wasn't enough for the American console. And we stayed in Austria quite a number of years. And during this time, he wrote me the most beautiful letters, which kept my spirits-- sent me food packages and clothing, and just maintain my spirits, like no one else.

speaker: interviewee
text: For some reason-- I was an orphan and a minor. And with all the documents and everything else, we went, even my aunt and I, undertook the trip to go to the American console general in Naples. And they refused a visa. I couldn't immigrate. This, to this date, will be something I will never understand. And indeed, I'm not a bitter person. But when I think that [? Castro ?] is opening his jail and lets people in, and here, after the war, I had to be in a DP camp and not to be let in-- and especially in view of the fact that somebody guaranteed my upkeep-- I cannot ever understand. And I never did come to the States. I went to Canada. I was taken to a transport to Canada, as a domestic, and emigrated to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your Aunt Rose with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My Aunt Rose, in the meantime, stayed on. Only young girls were needed and wanted. She stayed behind, in Italy. And she said she would eventually follow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that very hard for you, leaving her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was very hard to leave. But by then, I know that she is well, and that is only a matter of time. They told us that the elderly will follow later-- not elderly, but the ones who are older. They will follow later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where, exactly, did you go in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to-- my final destination was Montreal. We landed in Halifax. And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was 1949.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1949. From Genoa, we were taken by a ship, SS [INAUDIBLE], which was a warship. And we were taken on the ship. And I don't recall as thing, because I was sea sick from day one until we landed in Halifax. I always say, only my clothes marched out. I must have been left behind. But I did wind up in Halifax. And then by train, we were taken to Montreal. We were given badges. We were domestics, and we were requested by the government to work in Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you live and where did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to-- God must have looked down on me, because I was assigned to a lovely family, Dr. And Mrs. [? Levine ?] on [INAUDIBLE] Avenue, in Montreal-- who, indeed, took me in as their own daughter. And I never had to do, quote unquote, chores. They treated me like their daughter. And he's the first one who got me back on the medical field. He got, eventually, got me a position in the Jewish general hospital in Montreal, where he was, himself, one of the physicians. And I worked in the office of the hospital for about a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how was your health now, at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: At this point, I was fine. I had only one problem, which remains with me to this day. And this is why they never let me work. I have a chronic dislocation of both my shoulders, which happened in Auschwitz. They made us carry, at one time, must have been food, to the best of my recollection. And they came in these awful, big-- they are not cans. I don't know, like a barrel with handles, something like that. And my arm slipped out. And obviously, I was skin and bones and the girls were able to push it in. But that was the beginning. And it came out several times, after. And each time it came, it was more difficult. And when I came to Canada, and I told Dr. [? Levine ?], and he said he will not let me do any work. He will let me-- get me the position in the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop to change tapes here.
